name: Holy Roman Empire
note: Neither holy, Roman, nor an empire — Voltaire. A decentralized confederation of German princes, bishops, and city-states under an elected emperor. German princes systematically resisted centralisation; the Peace of Westphalia (1648) effectively made the HRE a loose alliance of sovereign states.

policies.name: Golden Bull of 1356
policies.description: Codified the seven prince-electors who chose the emperor, institutionalising fragmentation and limiting imperial authority.

policies.name: Peace of Westphalia
policies.description: Ended the Thirty Years' War; recognised princely sovereignty and religious tolerance. The founding document of the modern state system.

figures.name: Otto I the Great
figures.significance: Defeated the Magyars at Lechfeld (955); crowned emperor by the pope 962, founding the HRE

figures.name: Frederick I Barbarossa
figures.significance: The iconic medieval emperor; fought the Italian communes and the Lombard League; died on the Third Crusade

figures.name: Frederick II
figures.significance: Stupor Mundi — spoke six languages, patronised science, negotiated Jerusalem back through diplomacy rather than war; the most remarkable medieval ruler

figures.name: Charles V
figures.significance: Ruled the HRE, Spain, Naples, and the Americas simultaneously; presided over the Reformation crisis; abdicated exhausted

name: Kingdom of France
note: Capetian (987–1328), Valois (1328–1589), Bourbon (1589–1792) — three dynasties but one continuous institution. The most powerful western European monarchy of the High and Late Middle Ages. Absolute monarchy reached its apogee under Louis XIV.

policies.name: Edict of Nantes
policies.description: Henry IV granted Huguenots religious freedom and political rights — pragmatic end to the Wars of Religion. Revoked by Louis XIV in 1685, driving 200,000 Protestants into exile.

figures.name: Hugh Capet
figures.significance: Founded the Capetian dynasty that ruled France continuously for 800 years

figures.name: Philip II Augustus
figures.significance: Tripled royal territory; defeated the Plantagenets and Holy Roman Empire at Bouvines (1214) — the battle that made France the dominant western power

figures.name: Louis IX (Saint Louis)
figures.significance: The ideal medieval Christian king; reformed justice, lived ascetically, led two crusades; canonised 1297

figures.name: Joan of Arc
figures.significance: Turned the Hundred Years' War; her voices inspired Charles VII's reconquest of France from England; burned by the English, canonised 1920

figures.name: Louis XIV
figures.significance: The Sun King; ruled 72 years personally; built Versailles; made France the cultural hegemon of Europe; 'L'état, c'est moi'

name: Kingdom of England
note: Founded by Æthelstan; the Norman Conquest of 1066 replaced the Anglo-Saxon ruling class with a French-speaking Norman aristocracy, creating the distinctive Anglo-Norman culture. By 1300 English had re-emerged as the dominant tongue with heavy French vocabulary.

policies.name: Magna Carta
policies.description: First document placing the king under the rule of law; basis for English constitutional tradition and eventually parliamentary government.

policies.name: English Reformation
policies.description: Henry VIII broke with Rome to annul his marriage; the Church of England was established with the monarch as head — less theological than the Continental Reformation, more political.

figures.name: Alfred the Great
figures.significance: Defended Wessex against the Great Heathen Army; unified the English against the Vikings; laid the foundation for the unified Kingdom of England

figures.name: William I the Conqueror
figures.significance: Norman duke who conquered England at Hastings (1066); transformed English society, language, and architecture overnight

figures.name: Edward I
figures.significance: Conquered Wales, attempted conquest of Scotland; reformed English law; expelled the Jews (1290)

figures.name: Henry V
figures.significance: Won Agincourt (1415) against impossible odds; by Treaty of Troyes was heir to the French throne — died at 35 with it nearly in reach

figures.name: Elizabeth I
figures.significance: Elizabethan golden age; defeated the Spanish Armada (1588); presided over Shakespeare, Marlowe, and the birth of English colonialism

name: Kingdom of Great Britain
note: The Acts of Union 1707 merged England and Scotland into a single kingdom with one parliament. Scotland retained its distinct legal and church systems.

figures.name: Robert Walpole
figures.significance: First de facto Prime Minister of Great Britain; established the convention of cabinet government

name: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
note: Acts of Union 1800 added Ireland; the Irish Free State left in 1922, creating the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

figures.name: Duke of Wellington
figures.significance: Defeated Napoleon at Waterloo; later Prime Minister

figures.name: Queen Victoria
figures.significance: Ruled 63 years; the British Empire reached its greatest extent under her reign

name: Kingdom of Scotland
note: Kenneth MacAlpin united the Picts and Gaels. Over centuries the court Normanised and Scots (a Germanic dialect) replaced Gaelic as the lowland tongue, while Gaelic persisted in the Highlands.

figures.name: Kenneth MacAlpin
figures.significance: United the Pictish and Gaelic kingdoms; traditionally the first King of Scotland

figures.name: William Wallace
figures.significance: Led Scottish resistance against Edward I after Stirling Bridge (1297); executed in London; became Scotland's enduring national hero

figures.name: Robert I the Bruce
figures.significance: Won Scottish independence at Bannockburn (1314); the Declaration of Arbroath (1320) is among the earliest assertions of popular sovereignty

figures.name: Mary Queen of Scots
figures.significance: Catholic queen deposed by Protestant lords; imprisoned by Elizabeth I for 19 years; executed — her son James VI inherited England as James I

name: Kingdom of Denmark
note: The dominant Scandinavian power through the Middle Ages; ruled England 1013–1042 (Cnut); lost Sweden definitively in 1658; retains Greenland and Faroe Islands.

figures.name: Harald Bluetooth
figures.significance: United the Danish tribes; converted Denmark to Christianity; the Bluetooth wireless protocol is named after him

figures.name: Cnut the Great
figures.significance: Simultaneously ruled England, Denmark, and Norway — the North Sea Empire; the most powerful ruler of his era

figures.name: Margaret I
figures.significance: Architect of the Kalmar Union; the only woman to rule all three Scandinavian kingdoms simultaneously

name: Kingdom of Sweden
note: Broke from the Kalmar Union in 1523 under Gustav Vasa; became a major European Great Power in the 17th century (Swedish Empire, Stormaktstiden); lost Finland to Russia in 1809.

figures.name: Gustav I Vasa
figures.significance: Led rebellion against the Kalmar Union; founded modern Sweden; introduced the Lutheran Reformation; established the Vasa dynasty

figures.name: Gustav II Adolf (Gustavus Adolphus)
figures.significance: The Lion of the North; military genius who made Sweden a Great Power; died at Lützen championing Protestantism in the Thirty Years' War

figures.name: Charles XII
figures.significance: Brilliant but reckless; defeated Russia, Poland, and Denmark separately before losing to Peter the Great at Poltava (1709) — ending Sweden's Great Power status

name: Kingdom of Norway
note: Harald Fairhair unified Norway c.872. Norway entered personal union with Denmark in 1380 after the Oldenburg succession; effectively under Danish rule until 1814.

figures.name: Harald Fairhair
figures.significance: First King of Norway; his centralisation drove many Norse families to settle Iceland and the Atlantic islands

figures.name: Olaf II Haraldsson (Saint Olaf)
figures.significance: Completed Norway's Christianisation; killed at Stiklestad; became Norway's patron saint

figures.name: Haakon IV Haakonsson
figures.significance: Peak of medieval Norwegian power; incorporated Iceland and Greenland; died following the Battle of Largs against Scotland

name: Kalmar Union
note: Personal union of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden under one crown, dominated by Denmark. Sweden repeatedly rebelled; the Stockholm Bloodbath of 1520 (Danish massacre of Swedish nobles) triggered the final Swedish revolt under Gustav Vasa.

figures.name: Margaret I
figures.significance: Created the union in 1397 at Kalmar; the only person to successfully govern all three Scandinavian kingdoms

name: Kingdom of Hungary
note: Founded by Stephen I who received a crown from Pope Sylvester II. Medieval Hungary was one of the largest European kingdoms, stretching from the Adriatic to Transylvania. The Battle of Mohács (1526) ended the medieval kingdom; Hungary was split between Ottoman and Habsburg rule.

figures.name: Stephen I (Saint Stephen)
figures.significance: Christianised Hungary, received a crown from Rome, and transformed nomadic Magyar tribal society into a medieval Christian kingdom; patron saint of Hungary

figures.name: John Hunyadi
figures.significance: Defender of Christendom against the Ottomans; won the Battle of Belgrade (1456); his son became Matthias Corvinus

figures.name: Matthias Corvinus
figures.significance: Renaissance king; built the Bibliotheca Corviniana, one of Europe's greatest libraries; centralised power and modernised Hungary to its medieval peak

name: Habsburg Monarchy
note: The House of Habsburg held the HRE throne almost continuously 1438–1806. After Napoleon dissolved the HRE, the Habsburgs created the Austrian Empire (1804), then the Austro-Hungarian Dual Monarchy (1867–1918). 'Others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry' — AEIOU policy of dynastic expansion through marriage.

policies.name: Ausgleich (Compromise of 1867)
policies.description: Created the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary, granting Hungary equal status. Attempt to manage growing nationalism within the empire — worked for 50 years, then collapsed in WWI.

figures.name: Charles V
figures.significance: The apex of Habsburg power — ruled Spain, HRE, Italy, and the Americas; presided over the Reformation; abdicated exhausted

figures.name: Maria Theresa
figures.significance: Defended her inheritance against Frederick the Great; modernised the Habsburg state; mother of 16 children including Marie Antoinette and Joseph II

figures.name: Joseph II
figures.significance: Enlightened despot; abolished serfdom, granted religious toleration, reformed the church and judiciary — too fast for his subjects; most reforms reversed after his death

figures.name: Franz Joseph I
figures.significance: Ruled 68 years; the anchor of the old European order; his assassination's response to Archduke Franz Ferdinand's murder set off WWI

name: Kingdom of Bohemia
note: Přemyslid dynasty (895–1306), then Luxembourg (1310–1437). Bohemia became the intellectual centre of the HRE under Charles IV. The Hussite movement (1419–1434) was the first successful proto-Protestant revolt in Europe, a century before Luther.

figures.name: Wenceslas I (Good King Wenceslas)
figures.significance: Patron saint of Bohemia; murdered by his brother; Christianised Bohemia's court culture

figures.name: Charles IV
figures.significance: Also HRE Emperor; made Prague the imperial capital; founded Charles University (1348); issued the Golden Bull; the peak of Bohemian prestige

figures.name: Jan Hus
figures.significance: Theologian and church reformer burned at Constance; his execution triggered the Hussite Wars — a century before the Reformation, Bohemia was already there

name: Kingdom of Poland
note: Piast dynasty (966–1370), then Jagiellonian (1386–1569). Mieszko I's baptism in 966 is the founding moment of the Polish state. The Union of Lublin (1569) transformed the Polish-Lithuanian personal union into a real Commonwealth.

figures.name: Mieszko I
figures.significance: First historical ruler of Poland; his baptism in 966 placed Poland in the Latin Christian world and is the founding date of the Polish state

figures.name: Casimir III the Great
figures.significance: 'Found Poland wood, left it stone' — built dozens of castles and towns; codified Polish law; welcomed Jewish refugees fleeing Western European pogroms

figures.name: Jadwiga
figures.significance: First female King of Poland; married Jogaila of Lithuania, creating the personal union that would become the largest state in Europe

name: Grand Duchy of Lithuania
note: The last pagan state in Europe, Christianised only in 1387 when Jogaila married Jadwiga of Poland. At its peak the Grand Duchy stretched from the Baltic to the Black Sea, encompassing most of modern Ukraine and Belarus. Its administrative language was Ruthenian (Old East Slavic), not Lithuanian.

figures.name: Mindaugas
figures.significance: First and only King of Lithuania; united the Baltic tribes; briefly converted to Christianity for political reasons; assassinated by rivals

figures.name: Algirdas
figures.significance: Greatest territorial expansionist; pushed Lithuanian rule deep into Rus, reaching Crimea; a pagan ruling over a mostly Orthodox population

figures.name: Vytautas the Great
figures.significance: Defeated the Teutonic Knights at Grunwald (1410) alongside Poland; greatest Lithuanian ruler; at his peak controlled more territory than any contemporary European monarch

name: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
note: The Rzeczpospolita — an elected monarchy (Sejm elected the king) with the liberum veto, which allowed any single noble to dissolve the Sejm. The world's first country with a modern written constitution (May 3, 1791). Partitioned three times by Russia, Prussia, and Austria (1772, 1793, 1795) — erased from the map for 123 years.

policies.name: Liberum Veto
policies.description: Any single Sejm deputy could dissolve the parliament and veto all legislation. Increasingly paralysed government in the 18th century; exploited by Russia and Prussia to prevent Commonwealth reform.

policies.name: Constitution of 3 May 1791
policies.description: First modern written constitution in Europe; abolished the liberum veto, created a hereditary constitutional monarchy. Russia and Prussia invaded to destroy it — the Second Partition followed.

figures.name: Sigismund II Augustus
figures.significance: Last Jagiellonian; sealed the Union of Lublin (1569) transforming the personal union into a real Commonwealth with shared parliament

figures.name: Jan III Sobieski
figures.significance: Led the relief of Vienna (1683) against the Ottoman siege — the largest cavalry charge in history; saved Christian Europe but Poland gained little from it

name: Teutonic Order (Monastic State)
note: Crusading military order invited to Christianise the pagan Old Prussians; completed the conquest by 1283 (exterminating or assimilating the indigenous Prussians). At their peak controlled the entire Baltic coast from Danzig to Estonia. Catastrophically defeated at Grunwald (1410) by Poland-Lithuania; dissolved its monastic state and became a secular Duchy of Prussia in 1525.

figures.name: Hermann von Salza
figures.significance: Grand Master who organised the Baltic conquest; diplomatically shrewd — kept the Order independent between pope and emperor

figures.name: Ulrich von Jungingen
figures.significance: Grand Master killed at Grunwald (1410) — the Order's catastrophic defeat that ended its dominance of the Baltic

name: Kievan Rus
note: Founded by Varangian (Norse) Rurikids who were rapidly absorbed into Slavic culture. Christianisation under Vladimir I in 988 tied Rus to Constantinople and Eastern Orthodoxy — the defining civilisational choice. Fragmented into competing principalities after Yaroslav's death; destroyed by the Mongol invasion 1237–1241.

figures.name: Rurik
figures.significance: Legendary Varangian founder of the Rurikid dynasty; invited to rule Novgorod; his descendants ruled Russia until 1598

figures.name: Vladimir I the Great
figures.significance: Baptised himself and all of Kiev in the Dnieper in 988 — the Christianisation of Rus. Chose Orthodoxy over Catholicism, Islam, or Judaism after famously sending envoys to observe each faith's worship

figures.name: Yaroslav the Wise
figures.significance: Peak of Kievan Rus; codified the Rus Pravda (first law code); built Saint Sophia in Kiev; married his daughters to the kings of France, Hungary, and Norway

name: German Empire (Deutsches Reich)
note: Proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles after the Franco-Prussian War — deliberately humiliating for France. A Prussian-dominated constitutional monarchy; industrialised at breakneck speed to become Europe's foremost industrial power. Bismarck's system of alliances held Europe in balance until Wilhelm II dismissed him in 1890.

figures.name: Otto von Bismarck
figures.significance: Iron Chancellor; unified Germany through three wars (Denmark, Austria, France); created the first modern welfare state; his alliance system kept the peace — until Wilhelm II dismissed him

figures.name: Wilhelm II
figures.significance: Dismissed Bismarck; pursued Weltpolitik (global ambition); alienated Britain, France, and Russia simultaneously; led Germany into WWI; abdicated 1918

name: Angevin Empire
note: The Plantagenet dynasty empire under Henry II (who married Eleanor of Aquitaine), controlling Normandy, Anjou, Maine, Touraine, Poitou, and Aquitaine across the Channel plus England. A cross-Channel feudal aggregate rather than a unified state — conflict with the French crown over the vassal relationship was constant. John's loss of Normandy and Angevin lands in France (1202–1204) ended the continental empire; England persisted as the Plantagenet realm.

figures.name: Henry II Plantagenet
figures.significance: Built the Angevin Empire through inheritance and marriage to Eleanor of Aquitaine; ruled more French territory than the King of France himself; married his daughters into European royalty

figures.name: John Lackland (Jean sans Terre)
figures.significance: Lost Normandy and the Angevin lands to Philip II of France; his incompetence ended the continental empire. Forced to sign Magna Carta (1215); a byword for disastrous kingship

name: Kingdom of Italy
note: The Risorgimento unified the Italian peninsula for the first time since the Western Roman Empire. Italy joined WWI on the Allied side but felt cheated of territory at Versailles ('mutilated victory'), fuelling the rise of Fascism. Mussolini's regime allied with Nazi Germany; ended with Allied liberation and a 1946 referendum establishing the Republic.

figures.name: Giuseppe Garibaldi
figures.significance: Military hero of unification; his Expedition of the Thousand conquered the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies; the most celebrated Italian of his century

figures.name: Count Cavour
figures.significance: Political architect of Italian unification; Prime Minister of Piedmont who manoeuvred France and Prussia into enabling Italian independence

figures.name: Benito Mussolini
figures.significance: Invented Fascism; seized power 1922; allied with Hitler; Italy invaded Ethiopia and Greece; executed by partisans 1945 — the first Fascist dictator, killed by his own people
